records.ety: [F. lanterne, L. lanterna, laterna, from Gr. lampth`r light, torch. See Lamp.]

defs: Something inclosing a light, and protecting it from wind, rain, etc.; -- sometimes portable, as a closed vessel or case of horn, perforated tin, glass, oiled paper, or other material, having a lamp or candle within; sometimes fixed, as the glazed inclosure of a street light, or of a lighthouse light.
An open structure of light material set upon a roof, to give light and air to the interior.
A cage or open chamber of rich architecture, open below into the building or tower which it crowns.
A smaller and secondary cupola crowning a larger one, for ornament, or to admit light; such as the lantern of the cupola of the Capitol at Washington, or that of the Florence cathedral.
A lantern pinion or trundle wheel. See Lantern pinion (below).

A kind of cage inserted in a stuffing box and surrounding a piston rod, to separate the packing into two parts and form a chamber between for the reception of steam, etc.; -- called also lantern brass.
A perforated barrel to form a core upon.

notes: ☞ Fig. 1 represents a hand lantern; fig. 2, an arm lantern; fig. 3, a breast lantern; -- so named from the positions in which they are carried.

records.ety: [Cf. F. lanterner to hang at the lamp post, fr. lanterne. See Lantern.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Lanterned (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Lanterning.]

defs: To furnish with a lantern; as, to lantern a lighthouse.
